text: growth of gambling that we have seen since the 1990s, the seeds of its growth were planted long before. Our Constructed Risk Societies In the form of liminality associated with gambling, logic functions side by side with passion; the rules of cause and effect cease to exist; luck, rather than hard work, reigns; and ordinary people are transformed in a magical moment into millionaires, champions – or homeless beggars. ‘Play mocks at work’, as Caillois says (2001: 45). The appeal is obvious: it shows us what could be, if only the world did not run by the normal rules. It is an invitation into another world, a world turned upside down. The craving for gambling, however, also speaks to something deeper concerning the implosion brought about by liminality in contemporary life. The hotness of liminal excitement and spectacle quickly turns into Baudrillard’s coldness of the simulacrum, where ‘Energies freeze in their own fire, they deter themselves’ (Baudrillard 1994: 40). In the hyper-reality captured by Baudrillard, every boundary between real and imaginary has been broken down. Implosion is eating up all the energies of the real, falling in upon itself, as everything becomes a simulation. The real is no longer, it can only be artificially produced. Seen from that perspective, permanent liminality is the ultimate destruction of the very possibility of meaning. The sociological relevance of the development we have sketched here is not confined to the gambling industry itself. It was Wall Street’s brand of ‘casino capitalism’ that crashed the American economy in 2008, paving the way for the global economic crisis we still live with. Not without reason, in Caillois’ discussion Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 165 of the ways in which the various types of games can be ‘corrupted’, the clear and obvious link between alea and the stock market economy is stressed. While lotteries and casinos are the evident examples of alea within what Caillois calls ‘cultural forms found at the margins of the social order’ (but as we have already seen, those margins are no longer marginal), speculation on stock markets is the single example listed of ‘institutional forms integrated into social life’ of alea (Caillois 2001: 54). One of the major themes in social theory during the last two decades is the notion that we live in a ‘risk society’, as argued most famously by Ulrich Beck. But was not life more risky and dangerous for our grandparents than for us? If today we live in a risk-society, and if this defines our historical epoch, it is perhaps first and foremost because we long for risks and actively seek them out: it is a self- imposed haunt, much more so than a social-empirical diagnosis of our objective living conditions. This development can be further illustrated by looking into another play phenomenon pertaining to the accelareting global entertainment industry: that of bungee jumping. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 7 From Liminal to Liminoid to Limivoid: Bungee Jumping and the Quest for Excitement in Contemporary Leisure ‘Today you can try your limit’ Free day in Queenstown, today you can try your limit! Options are: Bungee Jumping, Sky Diving, Gondola and Luge, Jet Boating, Parasailing, Paragliding, Canyon Swing – and much more !!! (Brochure description of Day 6 activities for a typical Adventure Tour in New Zealand, Christmas/New Year 2010/2011) On September 25, 2010, James Field made it into the record books, after completing a world record forty-two bungee jumps in one hour, repeatedly hurling himself from a platform fifty metres off the ground. Field, who smashed the existing record of nineteen jumps, said he had to concentrate hard as he leapt from the windswept ledge near Tower Bridge in central London. It was a case of focusing, because you’ve got a lot of blood rushing to your head ... It was a lot harder than I expected but you just have to get into the rhythm. I was having to get my breath back each time I went back up. Mary Oladapo, who was monitoring the Sheffield man’s effort for Guinness World Records, said: ‘He was looking a bit queasy by the end and I don’t blame him’. Despite the time constraints, safety was a priority. After each leap, the crane that Mr Field was attached to lowered him to the ground, where he was caught by his assistants. The ten metre bungee rope was then changed before the crane took him back up to the platform. This chapter is about bungee jumping and how it reflects the world in which we live. During the 1980s bungee jumping became one of the most popular ways of seeking a ‘real experience’, when the practice was commercialized and introduced as a leisure activity around the world. This happened in the same period as outdoor sport activities or ‘outdoor recreation’ exploded in kinds and numbers, clearly linked to the ‘experiential turn in tourism’ and the proliferation of ‘adventure tourism’. This development has only been reinforced since, with adventure tourism establishing itself as a widely popular category, together with a proliferating number of sub-disciplines, including rafting, snowcross, paragliding, Liminality and the Modern 168 skysurfing, cliff diving, paintballing, drifting and base jumping – to name but a few. The more dangerous of these are often termed ‘extreme sports’. A key feature of these activities is the experience of danger: going to the limits, or indeed, standing on the limit. Hence, bungee jumping can be seen as an apt metaphor for understanding liminality in contemporary tourism and leisure. We live in an ‘adventure’ culture, or a ‘kick culture’ (Boeve 1999). The original meaning of the term adventure still clings to it today, as in mediaeval French it referred to ‘chance’ or ‘luck’. In Latin (adventure) the word referred to something about to happen, a future event, about whose outcome one could not know, a future participle of advenire. The term therefore involved the idea of venire, which indicates a movement, but also an arrival. In
